excerpt_en: June 27. Day 7 since arriving in the US. The new art culture of "Masu Photo" — taking photos while holding FOMUS masu or wearing masu accessories — has officially started in Los Angeles. This time I shot with Daria, an active model. We chose The Broad in downtown LA, a tourist spot. I also booked Yayoi Kusama in advance. Honestly, after going in, my first thought was, "Huh? This level??"
body_en:
June 27

So, it has been 7 days since I arrived in America.

Holding FOMUS masu,

or wearing masu accessories and taking photos —

the new art culture called "Masu Photo"

has officially started in Los Angeles. ✨

This time, I did a Masu Photo shoot with

Daria, who is an active model!

The location was The Broad, which I visited the other day.

It is downtown and also a tourist spot,

so I chose this place.

*It takes 1 hour from North Hollywood though.

Also, I had already booked Yayoi Kusama online in advance, which I couldn’t see last time!

I went in, and to be honest,

my first thought was,

"Huh? This level??"

I had zero prior info,

so I was expecting something much bigger,

but after experiencing teamLab,

I wasn’t particularly moved.

I felt there is much stronger entertainment out there.

Even so, tons of people are coming to see it,

so I once again felt the power of Yayoi Kusama.

And I did a bit of shooting inside The Broad as well.

It turned out to be a very meaningful Masu Photo session!

The FOMUS brand will be discovered from here.

I have absolutely no connections to models in LA,

but "making it happen" is my role

as the representative of FOMUS.

Masu Photo is art that cannot be created by one person,

and it cannot exist without both a photographer and a model —

content that AI can never replicate.

Right now, the era is AI,

and lots of people are using it.

I am one of them too.

Going forward, digital content will be

overflowing with AI-made output.

That makes it harder for AI output to gain value.

That is exactly why, at FOMUS,

we are preparing to build a community

that can create value across borders,

through "non-verbal" and "human-to-human connections,"

like the Sagishima Art Gallery (in progress)

and collaborations with masu.

AI cannot create

human connections,

land, time (history), or memories.

FOMUS activities are built with those in mind,

using value that AI cannot produce.

Masu Photo, in particular,

is packed with all of that.

Because I fly around and shoot,

we shoot in each place,

and we share the time of that moment (when it was taken)

and the memories.

And because we took these Masu Photos,

when they come to Japan,

they can meet and interact with FOMUS community members,

and the reverse pattern is also possible!

This culture of connecting through masu

won’t generate money right away,

but in the near future,

the FOMUS brand

will likely be seen as interesting

by many companies.

"If you leave it to FOMUS,

they will do something fun!"

If people start thinking like that,

then because FOMUS is a unique community

of creators, artists, models, and everyday people,

all holding the traditional Japanese craft of masu,

there are things we can do that others cannot.

Over a long time,

we will create a culture and a community

that others cannot easily imitate.

excerpt_en: June 28. In the morning, I did a Masu Photo shoot in Los Angeles with Claire, a Taiwanese student studying here. We shot on Santa Monica Third Street and at the Santa Monica Pier. Afterward, I spent happy hour at the pier, watching the crowd to understand who actually comes here. The takeaway: it’s mostly families and couples. So I raised my target age up to 50 and changed the copy to allow parent-child shoots.
body_en:
June 28

So, in the morning I did a Masu Photo shoot.

I shot with Claire, a Taiwanese student

studying in Los Angeles.

This time, we shot at Santa Monica

Third Street.

It is a pedestrian-only street,

with lots of boutiques lined up.

Shooting with this as the backdrop was pretty interesting.

Then we went to a tourist spot in Santa Monica,

the Santa Monica Pier.

It is a pier,

and lots of tourists gather there.

We shot there as well.

In about an hour, we got some great Masu Photos.

After that, we said goodbye, and I stayed there,

having happy hour at a shop on the pier. 🍻

While drinking beer,

I just kept watching the people there.

The reason is, over the last few days,

I’ve collected data that the customer-acquisition methods

that worked in Southeast Asia

aren’t working in Los Angeles.

To be honest, I expected that.

That’s why I needed to see for myself:

how do people gather here?

What kind of people actually come here?

There are so many things you can’t know without seeing them.

What I ended up learning was:

the people at Santa Monica Pier are

mostly families and couples.

It doesn’t seem like a place where people come alone.

So, for our approach,

I raised the target age

from 19–35 up to 50,

and changed the messaging to allow parent-child shoots.

Now I just have to see if this creates movement...

There’s no choice but to test it.

Groceries are expensive too

I went to a supermarket 10 minutes on foot

from the hotel I’m staying at.

If you eat out every day, money disappears fast,

so I cut costs wherever I can.

4 banana muffins for 5 dollars

Potato chips for 2 dollars

Two mystery ramen packs for 3 dollars

I bought those as sides for lunch and dinner.

Even that comes to about 1,600 yen,

so the weak yen is tough. Lol

Joined a pub crawl

I’m staying at a place about 5 minutes on foot

from Santa Monica Beach.

There was a flyer saying there’s a pub crawl on Fridays!

"That sounds fun!"

So I joined!

There were 16 people total,

all staying at the same hotel.

After brief self-introductions,

I realized I was the only Asian.

Of course, Japanese doesn’t work here,

so everything is in English.

Since it was a tour, we went to 3 pubs.

I socialized with people from Argentina, Venezuela, Mexico,

and a bunch of other countries.

Here, it seems normal to chat even with strangers sitting next to you,

so it’s a style of communication where you just talk a lot.

There’s no time to worry about your English being weak. Lol

Even so, from 8:00 PM to 11:30 PM,

I enjoyed drinking and talking.

By the way, beer is like $10 per glass,

so for 3 places, that’s about $30.

Three beers cost me about 5,000 yen. Lol

In Japan it’d be around 2,000 yen.

Still, these experiences and encounters

are once-in-a-lifetime,

and I felt it was a good way to spend money.

Save where you can,

and spend properly where it matters.

How you use money is a super important factor too.

excerpt_en: June 29. After enjoying the pub crawl, I woke up at 9. The nice thing about my hotel is the free breakfast. Bread, cereal, nuts, bananas, milk, and orange juice — a lifesaver with the weak yen. Then at 10 AM I did a Masu Photo shoot in Santa Monica. Afterward I stayed to explore a weekend event, and in the evening I did desk work at the hotel — editing photos, AI work, and FOMUS tasks.
body_en:
June 29

After the pub crawl and a fun night of drinking,

I got back to the hotel and woke up at 9.

The nice thing about the hotel I’m staying at is

the free breakfast.

Bread, cornflakes, nuts, bananas,

milk, and orange juice are all available.

With the weak yen and a tough life for Japanese travelers,

this is a huge help!!

Since arriving in Santa Monica,

I’ve been relying on it every day.

Then from 10:00, in Santa Monica,

Masu Photo shoot!

With Iz.

She likes Japanese culture and such,

and she applied to join the shoot!

It makes me happy to have people participate like this!

After we finished and said goodbye,

I stayed in Santa Monica to do some sightseeing.

Since it was Saturday, there was an event,

so I went to check it out!

From the evening,

I did desk work at the hotel.

Editing photos,

AI work and study,

and FOMUS work —

there’s always a huge pile of things to do,

so it’s not like I can just play all day

like a typical tourist. Lol

I guess that’s what being a nomad worker is like. Lol

excerpt_en: June 30. I had a Masu Photo shoot scheduled, but it got cancelled, so my daytime plans disappeared. I decided to run to Beverly Hills instead of taking a train or bus. From Santa Monica to Rodeo Drive is about 10 km. I arrived after stopping for breaks. That night I went to West Hollywood with Kenji, an opera writer I met here.
body_en:
June 30

I originally had a Masu Photo shoot scheduled,

but it got cancelled at the last minute,

so my daytime plans disappeared.

So I decided to go for marathon training.

I planned to take a train or bus to Beverly Hills,

a place I still hadn’t visited in Los Angeles,

but I chose to run instead!!

From Santa Monica to Rodeo Drive in Beverly Hills

is about 10 km.

It was hot, so I took breaks,

but I made it to Beverly Hills!

This area is lined with luxury brand stores,

so most people look like they have money.

I was the only one walking around in running clothes.

Looking at the storefronts of the luxury brands,

their colors and atmosphere are all different —

they really differentiate themselves.

At FOMUS, we’re currently building a gallery

on Sagishima in Hiroshima,

so this was a good study for me!

After that, I took a small detour

to the Beverly Hills sign.

It took quite a while,

but I ran and walked about 19 km.

It was great training!

To West Hollywood!

At night, I went to West Hollywood

with Kenji, an opera writer I met here.

This area has lots of bars where gay people gather.

This time I wanted to talk,

so we just enjoyed the atmosphere of the bars,

and chose somewhere quieter to drink.

After drinking until around 1 AM,

Kenji and I took different bus routes.

I also took a bus to go back! But

I was at the bus stop and the bus passed without stopping!

With the smell of marijuana in the air,

I waited about 20 minutes,

and finally the bus came. Lol

In the end, I got back around 2 AM. Lol

It turned into a day I fully enjoyed from morning to night!

excerpt_en: July 1. My last day of activities in Los Angeles. I had a 21:40 flight to Las Vegas, so I worked by the ocean in Santa Monica during the morning. Before the flight, I had a final dinner with Teeny and Kenji. We aimed to get to the airport by 19:30, but we arrived after 20:00. Checked baggage was no longer accepted, so I had to carry my suitcase on — and my lotions over 100 ml were thrown away. Then the gate required a bus transfer, and I ended up missing the flight. I booked a night bus instead and finally arrived in Las Vegas at 5 AM.
body_en:
July 1

Last day of activities in Los Angeles!

I had a 21:40 flight on July 1

and was planning to move to Las Vegas.

So in the morning, as a final Santa Monica moment,

I worked while looking at the ocean.

Then, before the flight,

in Los Angeles,

I went to a Thai restaurant for a final dinner

with Teeny and Kenji, who helped me so much.

We said we should get to the airport by 19:30

since we can’t be late for the flight.

It was a really fun time, but

they were taking it slow,

so I wondered if it was okay since it was a domestic flight,

but in the end we arrived at the airport after 20:00.

I had already checked in online, but in my case, I needed to check baggage.

When I went there,

they told me the service was already closed.

They said I could send it on another flight

or take it as carry-on.

I couldn’t do the scary option of another flight,

so I decided to carry it on.

But there was almost no time left before the flight.

And since I had to bring my suitcase as carry-on,

all my lotions in containers over 100 ml

got thrown away...

That hurt a lot.

They’re expensive.

But I couldn’t complain,

so I rushed to the gate as fast as I could.

Unlike Japanese airports,

after the security check,

you go to the boarding gate — but

there was a bus transfer!

And it took more than 10 minutes!

I finally got to the gate area and

ran with more than 30 kg of luggage to the gate!

When I arrived and tried to enter,

security said,

“The flight time is already over.”

Huh?

No way.

And just like that, I missed a flight for the first time in my life.

No way I could get to Las Vegas!!

I looked for another flight, but

there aren’t many LA → Las Vegas flights,

and the next one was two days later...

So it was clear a flight wouldn’t make it,

and I booked a night bus ticket on the spot!

By this time, I was in contact with the dinner group,

so I told them I couldn’t catch the flight,

and they drove me from the airport to the bus stop!

Because it was a night bus,

the departure was at 23:00,

so this time I was determined not to be late,

and went to a beer bar near the bus stop! Lol

In the end, the night bus was also close, but

I somehow made it on!

When I missed the flight,

I wondered what to do, but

I already knew from experience that panicking is useless,

and I also knew about the night bus,

so I was able to move safely.

By the way,

after it was confirmed I wouldn’t be flying,

I went back from the gate to the security area,

and when I looked in the trash,

the thrown-away lotions were still there,

so I told the staff I wasn’t flying

and asked for them back.

They held them until I exited,

but they returned them.

I’m a bit opportunistic in these situations. Lol

They were expensive,

and I don’t trust overseas ones. Lol

And then, 5 AM.

I arrived in Las Vegas!

Bonus: me at the moment I missed the flight

excerpt_en: July 2. I covered missing the flight by taking an overnight bus and safely arrived in Las Vegas. From the bus stop to the Strip, I took another bus and bought an unlimited day pass online for $8 (about 1,300 yen). Until evening I did sightseeing — daytime Vegas doesn’t have much beyond hotel hopping and tourist spots. It was over 40°C, and everything was casinos. Food was expensive so I went to McDonald’s. In the evening I stayed at my Couchsurfing host George’s house — a private room! After a shower and a bento he prepared, I passed out from exhaustion.
body_en:
July 2

I covered missing the flight with an overnight bus

and safely arrived in Las Vegas!

From the bus stop

to the Strip with all the famous hotels,

I took a bus,

and bought a Las Vegas bus pass online!

Unlimited rides for 1 day: $8.

About 1,300 yen.

This day, I was staying with a Couchsurfing host,

and he asked me to come in the evening,

so I did Las Vegas sightseeing until then.

To be honest, during the day in Las Vegas,

there isn’t much to do,

so it’s mainly hotel hopping and tourist spots.

*I left my luggage at the Bellagio Hotel.

Las Vegas was over 40°C,

and it was insanely hot!!

Basically you walk around inside hotels,

and it’s casinos everywhere!

They’re literally everywhere.

I got hungry,

so I looked at the shops inside the hotels, but

of course, they were expensive.

I won’t splurge alone,

so I went to McDonald’s.

By the way, this cost about 1,500 yen.

It’s just expensive! Lol

Man, with this weak yen,

life in America is pretty tough.

Then evening came,

so I went to my host’s house.

But from the nearest bus stop,

it was a 20-minute walk!!

Carrying a ridiculously heavy suitcase,

a backpack, and a tote bag,

I walked in 40+ degree heat.

Honestly, it was really tough!

I took breaks many times,

and finally arrived!!

My host George greeted me!

Right after arriving, he showed me the room —

a private room!!

My first private room since coming to America!

I was a little moved. Lol

And I was drenched in sweat,

so I immediately borrowed the shower.

George had prepared a bento lunch.

I had eaten McDonald’s for lunch, but

I burned all that off on the way here,

so I accepted it, of course.

No photo, but

it was a fish bento, and it was delicious!

He also said, “I’ll show you Las Vegas at night!”

But after an overnight bus,

hotel hopping from the morning,

and walking through the scorching heat,

I was completely exhausted.

I planned to sleep for an hour,

but ended up sleeping for about three.

So there was no night tour, but

George took me by car to his recommended burger spot,

and we did drive-through!

I enjoyed it!!

I never thought I’d eat two burgers in one day. Lol

*In America, just having food is something to be grateful for.

*The weak yen is rough.

I’ll be in Las Vegas for 3 nights and 4 days,

but one of those days was spent on the night bus.

Day 2 is at George’s house,

and day 3 is planned at a hotel, so

I won’t have that much time with George,

but I want to talk as much as possible!

excerpt_en: July 3. From 8 AM, George drove me about 30 minutes to Red Rock Canyon. It’s a national conservation area with red rock mountains and desert plants and animals, and you can tour it by car. We stopped at points for photos — it was great and highly recommended. After Thai food, he dropped me at the Bellagio. I finally understood how fun Couchsurfing is and learned that the host makes a big difference. Then I did a Masu Photo shoot at the Bellagio’s indoor garden and headed to the Sphere. The visuals and sound were amazing, though the story didn’t fully click for me. I checked into the Bellagio, the only five-star hotel on this trip, and explored its casino and shows. I even played a little — drinks are free while gambling — and ended the night tipsy.
body_en:
July 3

From 8 AM, George took me by car

to Red Rock Canyon, about 30 minutes away.

Red Rock Canyon is a national conservation area,

with red rock mountains and desert plants and animals.

And the nice thing is you can tour it by car!

You park at a sightseeing point, take photos,

then move on by car — that kind of flow.

It was a really fun time!

I highly recommend this place!

Then we went straight to lunch.

We went into a Thai restaurant.

Man, it was really delicious!

I also ordered iced coffee.

But it was about $20.

That’s 3,200 yen. Lol

This is where the weak yen is scary.

Money disappears in an instant...

Then George took me

to the Bellagio Hotel where I’d be staying this time.

It was a short time, but

being able to talk and connect was super fun,

and I finally felt how interesting Couchsurfing is!

This was only my second host, but I learned

that your satisfaction changes a lot depending on the host.

Masu Photo shoot

I arrived at the Bellagio Hotel,

left my luggage with the bell desk,

and immediately did a Masu Photo shoot!

I had been running ads on Instagram in Las Vegas,

and I was able to connect with someone living here.

The shoot location was the indoor garden of the Bellagio.

To be honest, it was too hot outside to shoot. Lol

Even so, we got good photos!

To the Sphere

Now, does the schedule end here? Nope.

From here, I went to the entertainment facility

called the “Sphere,” which opened last year.

It’s a giant sphere where both the inside and outside

are made entirely of LED screens.

The purpose of this Las Vegas trip was

the Sphere and Cirque du Soleil,

so I’m glad I got to see it.

Still, the visuals and sound were amazing, but

the story of the visuals didn’t really click for me,

and it ended with “the visuals are insane!”

Live concerts seem like they’d be amazing there.

It’s hard to describe the Sphere in words,

so please experience it for yourself at least once!

Bellagio Hotel

I checked into the only five-star hotel on this US trip,

the Bellagio Hotel.

This was the one place I really wanted to stay,

and since I saved money on lodging through Couchsurfing,

I decided to stay here.

A massive casino, Cirque du Soleil facilities,

a huge pool, high-brand shops — it has everything.

I walked around the pool, the casino, and various areas,

and it’s just gorgeous.

And basically, most people are families or couples.

I didn’t see any solo guys.

Even staying in the room, there wasn’t much to do,

so I watched the super famous Bellagio fountains

and the volcano show at the hotel next door.

Then I played a little at the casino.

In American casinos,

drinks are free only while you’re playing.

At first, I didn’t know this,

so I ordered beer at a different hotel

thinking it would be free, and got charged $14.

Learning from that,

at the Bellagio I drank while gambling.

That said,

I was still playing at the casino,

so it felt like I was paying for the beer anyway.

I didn’t feel like I could win at all that day,

so I just had a little fun with a small amount.

In exchange, I drank plenty,

got nicely tipsy,

and went back to my room to sleep.

This is a trick you can only do if you’re staying there,

and part of the appeal of being able to play late into the night!

But one thing I can say is,

staying at the Bellagio alone as a guy

isn’t very fun.

You really need to come with someone! Lol

excerpt_en: July 4. Last day in Las Vegas. I wanted to relax until the last minute, so I set the meeting point for Masu Photo 20 minutes after checkout — but checkout had a huge line. I left my suitcase with the bell desk and made it to the shoot. The second Vegas model was Javry, a very tall model. We shot in the Bellagio garden and at the pool area. After that another shoot got cancelled, so I ate McDonald’s. The self-ordering kiosks charged me but didn’t print a receipt at first, so I might have paid twice. Then I watched Cirque du Soleil “O.” I bought a first-floor seat and it cost 40,000 yen — prices were up due to the weak yen and the U.S. Independence Day. The show was incredible. Fireworks were visible from the Bellagio. On the way back to the airport, buses were badly delayed, so I took an Uber for 6,500 yen.
body_en:
July 4

Top-tier entertainment

Today is the last day in Las Vegas.

It was only three days, so it went by so fast.

I had a relaxed morning at the Bellagio,

and my Masu Photo shoot was at 11:20.

I wanted to stay in the hotel as long as possible,

so I set the meeting point for 20 minutes after checkout time.

But something unexpected happened.

Forget check-in — there was a line for checkout!!

There was no way I could check out in 20 minutes! Lol

So I left only my suitcase with the bell desk,

and delayed checkout.

Thanks to that, I made it to the shoot safely.

*I checked out after the shoot.

Masu Photo with a professional model

The second Masu Photo model in Las Vegas was

Javry, a super tall model.

She came with her boyfriend,

and he was about my height.

And she was born in the Philippines.

This time as well, we shot in the Bellagio garden,

and I had checked the day before that

the pool area also had good shooting spots,

so we took photos there too.

The shoot was calm and friendly from start to finish.

Even on a blonde adult woman,

the Masu accessory “FOMUS Parure” looks super cool,

so I think it deserves more recognition!

For that, I’m gathering materials now,

and I’ll launch promotions at the right time.

Lunch problem

After the shoot,

I had another one scheduled, but it got cancelled, so I went to lunch.

Honestly, America is expensive everywhere.

So when I eat alone, I save wherever I can.

Not healthy, but I went to McDonald’s.

You order on a touchscreen, but

for some reason, even after paying, no receipt with a number came out!

I ordered again on a different touchscreen and a receipt came out.

I paid by credit card, so

maybe I paid for two meals.

These are the kinds of struggles that are different from Japan.

A burger you can eat for about 600 yen in Japan

is easily over 1,000 yen here.

If McDonald’s is like this,

other places are even more expensive,

so it’s pretty tough.

Went to see Cirque du Soleil

On the last day in Las Vegas,

I watched Cirque du Soleil “O.”

I wanted to see it from the first floor,

so I bought a ticket — and it was 40,000 yen.

Because of the weak yen,

and because it was U.S. Independence Day,

the ticket prices were inflated.

I went without doing any research, but

the performance was overwhelming — I was deeply moved!!

The combination of high-quality performance and comedy

was crafted to keep the audience from getting bored.

Above all, the stage set was incredible!

The stage can change the water level instantly,

so it can wow you with synchronized swimming

and then make it possible to run across the stage.

This mechanism is just insane,

and absolutely worth seeing!

FOMUS is entertainment too, even if the genre is different.

Being able to experience top-tier entertainment

was a great learning experience!

And as I wrote earlier,

it was U.S. Independence Day.

I could see fireworks from the Bellagio,

and lots of people were wearing American flag outfits!

I’m glad I was in Las Vegas during this festival period!

On the other hand, hotel and theater costs were high,

so once again, the weak yen is tough. Lol

Getting to the airport was rough

On the way back, I used Uber.

I wanted to take a $6 bus from the hotel to the airport,

but the city was in party mode that day,

and the buses were heavily delayed.

I thought I might miss another flight,

so I used Uber instead.

It was about a 15-minute ride,

and it cost 6,500 yen!!

So expensive!!

This is America’s pricing.

Honestly, coming from Japan,

America feels like a much higher hurdle now.

I need to earn foreign currency properly!

excerpt_en: This US trip had three stops: Los Angeles, Las Vegas, and New York. I planned to stay in New York for 10 nights and 11 days to build a foothold for expanding the FOMUS brand. I used Couchsurfing again to save on lodging and connect with locals. On the way from the airport to downtown, the rubber on my suitcase wheels (used for about 9 years) broke. With about 25 kg of luggage, moving became brutal, but I managed. I went to Chris’s place (a nursing student) for three nights, visited the Met and The Cloisters, and ended the day with McDonald’s and watching Jujutsu Kaisen.
body_en:
This US trip has three locations:

Los Angeles, Las Vegas,

and New York 🗽

This time, I’m staying in New York for 10 nights and 11 days,

so it’s a bit of a longer stay.

The reason is

to build a foothold for expanding the FOMUS brand.

Honestly, I don’t think I can enter America’s “village society”

in just a few weeks, but my mindset is to do everything I can.

This time as well, by using Couchsurfing,

I can save on lodging costs

and also connect with people living locally.

From the airport to downtown

I arrived in New York,

and to get to downtown,

you usually take a monorail-like train,

transfer to a regular train,

or use Uber or a taxi.

But Uber is too expensive,

so trains only!

So, I moved that way.

However—

the rubber on the wheels of my overseas suitcase,

which I’d used for about 9 years, broke!!

Probably because from May to June,

I used it almost every day to travel around Japan,

and then in Las Vegas, I dragged it over scorching hot roads

for about 20 minutes—

the rubber got shredded...

Carrying about 25 kg of luggage

became insanely hard.

Two wheels were barely still alive,

so I managed by using it like a two-wheeler.

But this won’t last long...

I have to fix it soon!

After that hard travel,

I somehow made it onto the train.

America is huge, so

the way you ride trains differs between

Los Angeles, Las Vegas, and New York.

The TAP card I used in LA

doesn’t work in New York or Las Vegas.

By the way, Apple Pay works in New York, so if you register it, you’re good!

So,

on the first day, I went to Chris’s house

where I’d be staying for three days.

It was, surprisingly, a medical center!

Chris is a nursing student,

and he rents out his room.

I stayed there for 3 nights.

First, he explained the room rules,

and asked if I was hungry,

so he made me oatmeal!

*No photo

Then Chris recommended some spots.

One was the Metropolitan Museum of Art,

one of the world’s top three museums.

Apparently there are two museums

and you can enter both with the same ticket.

First, I walked 30 minutes to The Cloisters,

then went to the Met.

The Met has about 1.5 million works,

and I rushed through for about 4 hours,

but still didn’t have enough time! Lol

I got to see famous works by

Van Gogh and Monet!

After that I went back,

and since meals are basically individual,

I went to McDonald’s near the house.

Honestly, I only know how to save money by

cooking or going to McDonald’s!

You can eat at McDonald’s for under $10.

That’s under 1,600 yen.

That’s probably how I’ll gain weight. Lol

After getting back,

I did all my personal work that had piled up,

and watched Jujutsu Kaisen in my room! Lol

Anime culture really is amazing!!

And just like that, fun New York life begins!!

excerpt_en: Masu Photo in New York. I shot with Mari, a Japanese dancer aiming for Broadway who moved to the US alone and recently got an artist visa. We shot inside Grand Central and at Times Square — my first ever Times Square. After lunch at Junior’s (NY cheesecake), we talked about our activities. Later I looked for a hardware store to fix my suitcase, bought strong tape instead, and then went on a picnic with Chris and his nursing-school friend Daria. These Couchsurfing connections are exactly why this choice feels right.
body_en:
Masu Photo shoot in New York

So, in New York as well,

I actually did a Masu Photo shoot!

Today’s model was Mari, a Japanese woman.

She moved to America alone

to become a Broadway dancer, and is working here.

She even got a U.S. artist visa six months ago,

and is now active full-scale.

FOMUS is trying to go global,

and I’m here for that too, so even though our genres differ,

we’re both challenging the world.

So I did a Masu Photo shoot with her!

Inside Grand Central Station and at Times Square.

And of course—

my first time ever at Times Square!

I couldn’t go there on my graduation trip as a student,

so this was a long-held dream!

After the shoot, we had lunch together.

She introduced a recommended spot.

We went to Junior’s, famous for New York cheesecake.

We ordered a burger, baked onion soup,

and cheesecake!

Everything was delicious,

but the soup was especially good!!

*For some reason, it had cheese in it!!

I thought it would be cool to do something like this with masu.

True to American size, everything was huge,

but we shared and finished everything properly.

Including tip, it was $58.

So about 9,500 yen.

The weak yen is really tough. Lol

At lunch,

we were constantly talking about our activities.

Hearing real stories from someone taking auditions overseas

was super interesting and made me realize how high the level is.

Because we’re both challenging the world,

there was a lot we could talk about,

and it was a very meaningful time!

After we said goodbye,

I walked around Times Square a bit.

Then I looked for a hardware store

to fix my broken suitcase,

but I couldn’t find a rubber plate.

So I bought some tough-looking tape instead,

and I’m hoping I can survive this month with it. Lol

*As long as I make it to Ireland, I’ll be okay for now

To a picnic

I got a message from Chris,

and after returning home,

I went on a picnic in a nearby park

with him and Daria, who goes to the same nursing school.

She’s studying Japanese,

so she could speak a little, and I became her practice partner.

She’s Russian but lives in America.

These kinds of opportunities

are born precisely because of Couchsurfing,

so I really felt that

this choice wasn’t wrong.

*If you stay at a hotel, it’s hard to meet people.

*The Bellagio is a good example.

My life with Chris ends tomorrow!

Three days went by in a flash!
